stem: What does AWS recommend for human users that need access to AWS accounts?

options.text: Have humans sign in through federation or IAM Identity Center so they obtain temporary credentials rather than long-term IAM user credentials.
Create a long-lived IAM user with an access key for each engineer and require them to rotate the key on a schedule.
Share a single root user password across the team so administrators always have full access.
Issue console passwords only and disable role assumption to keep the access model uniform.

explanation: AWS best practices recommend that human users access AWS through federation or IAM Identity Center, which yields temporary credentials and centralizes identity management instead of relying on long-term IAM user credentials.

distractorExplanations.b: Long-term IAM user access keys for humans run counter to AWS guidance. Federation with temporary credentials is the recommended pattern, even when keys are rotated.
distractorExplanations.c: Sharing the root user defeats accountability and contradicts the guidance to protect and seldom use the root user.
distractorExplanations.d: Disabling role assumption removes the very mechanism federation relies on, since federated humans typically receive permissions through assumed roles.
